questions.questionText: A benign tumor of glandular epithelium is called an:
questions.explanation: Adenoma denotes a benign epithelial neoplasm from glands.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most reliable morphologic criterion distinguishing a malignant tumor from a benign one?
questions.explanation: Metastasis is the absolute hallmark of malignancy.

questions.questionText: Carcinoma in situ is defined as:
questions.optionB: Invasion of tumor cells into the underlying stroma
questions.optionC: A benign tumor with a high risk of malignant transformation
questions.optionD: Dysplasia involving the full thickness of the epithelium without breaching the basement membrane
questions.explanation: CIS has all cytological features of malignancy but lacks invasion.

questions.questionText: The p53 gene product functions primarily to:
questions.optionB: Promote progression from G2 to M phase
questions.optionC: Arrest the cell cycle in G1 to allow for DNA repair
questions.explanation: p53 'guardian of the genome' halts cell cycle to repair DNA, or triggers apoptosis if unrepairable.

questions.questionText: A classical example of a chemical carcinogen causing hepatocellular carcinoma is:
questions.explanation: Aflatoxins produced by Aspergillus contaminate grains and peanuts, causing HCC.

questions.questionText: Which virus is causally associated with Burkitt lymphoma?
questions.explanation: EBV is strongly associated with endemic Burkitt lymphoma.

questions.questionText: Rb (Retinoblastoma gene) strictly regulates the cell cycle transition from:
questions.explanation: Hypophosphorylated Rb binds and inhibits E2F, blocking G1 to S progression.

questions.questionText: The grading of a cancer is based on:
questions.optionA: The degree of cellular differentiation and mitotic activity
questions.optionB: The extent of spread to lymph nodes
questions.optionC: The size of the primary tumor
questions.explanation: Grading is histological (how abnormal the cells look). Staging is clinical/pathological (extent of spread).

questions.questionText: Which tumor marker is most useful for monitoring recurrence of colon cancer?
questions.explanation: CEA is elevated in colorectal carcinoma.

questions.questionText: Tumor cells often shift their metabolism to aerobic glycolysis (Warburg effect) to:
questions.optionA: Provide metabolic intermediates for cellular biosynthesis
questions.optionC: Maximize ATP production per glucose molecule
questions.explanation: The Warburg effect allows tumor cells to use carbon from glucose for rapid biomass synthesis.

questions.questionText: In Neoplasia, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Pathology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Pathology references for Neoplasia. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Neoplasia.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Neoplasia scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Neoplasia.
